planting.note: Plant seeds or container-grown palms in spring in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, sandy or loamy soil in full sun; triangle palm is highly drought tolerant once established and thrives in dry, fast-draining soils. Frost-sensitive — protect young plants below 28°F, established palms may tolerate brief 25°F dips.

directions: Plant in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, sandy or loamy soil in full sun; triangle palm is one of the most architecturally distinctive palms in the world — the fronds arise from the trunk arranged in three distinct ranks (rows), creating a perfectly triangular cross-section visible from above and giving the entire crown a dramatic, sharply geometric triangular silhouette unlike any other palm. Native to the dry, rocky spiny forest of southern Madagascar, it is exceptionally drought-tolerant and well-adapted to hot, dry, sandy soils. The stiff, strongly arched fronds are blue-gray-green and held erect, creating a bold, sculptural presence. One of the most popular ornamental palms for dry, warm tropical and subtropical gardens in California, Florida, Arizona, and similar climates. Not tolerant of wet, poorly drained soils. The triangular arrangement of leaves (tristichous phyllotaxy) is one of the most unusual and recognizable features of any landscape palm — a genuine conversation piece in the garden.

planting.note: Plant seeds or container-grown palms in spring in well-drained to moist, slightly acidic to neutral, sandy or loamy soil in full sun; Senegal date palm is adaptable to moist and occasionally flooded soils near streams, unlike most palms. More cold-hardy than other Phoenix species other than Canary Island palm — tolerating brief freezes to about 22°F.

directions: Plant in well-drained to moist, slightly acidic to neutral, sandy or loamy soil in full sun; Senegal date palm is the most important palm of tropical Africa — a naturally multi-trunked, suckering palm (producing clusters of arching trunks that grow to 25 feet and lean outward from a common base) native to riverbanks, lake margins, and moist savannas across tropical Africa from Senegal to Tanzania. The multi-stemmed, arching, clustering growth habit creates an instantly recognizable, lush tropical silhouette quite different from the single-trunked date palms. The small date-like fruits are edible, used locally for food and wine. More cold-hardy than most Phoenix species other than the Canary Island date palm, tolerating brief temperatures to about 22°F when mature and established — making it one of the hardiest of all fruiting palms. An outstanding ornamental for large spaces where its spreading, multi-stemmed habit can develop. Widely naturalized and invasive in Florida.

planting.note: Plant seeds or container-grown palms in spring in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, sandy or loamy soil in full sun; bottle palm is frost-sensitive (damaged below 32°F, killed below 25°F) and requires a truly frost-free climate. Extremely rare in the wild — only about 10 individuals survive in the wild on Round Island, Mauritius.

directions: Plant in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, sandy or loamy soil in full sun in frost-free zone 10–11; bottle palm is one of the world's most distinctive and beloved ornamental palms — the dramatically swollen, bottle-shaped trunk (widest near the base and narrowing dramatically as it rises) topped by a small crown of arching, pinnate fronds is one of the most instantly recognizable palm silhouettes. Native exclusively to Round Island, a small uninhabited island north of Mauritius in the Indian Ocean, where it is critically endangered — fewer than 10 wild individuals survive due to historical invasive species damage. However, it is widely cultivated throughout the tropical and subtropical world as an ornamental, with hundreds of thousands of cultivated specimens in gardens, parks, and streets. The bottle shape is most pronounced in younger trees, typically becoming less pronounced with age as the trunk elongates. Extremely salt-tolerant and well-adapted to sandy, dry, coastal conditions. One of the premier ornamental palms for South Florida, Hawaii, and similar frost-free climates.

planting.note: Plant seeds or container-grown palms in spring in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, sandy or loamy soil in full sun; spindle palm is frost-sensitive and requires frost-free conditions. Native to Rodrigues Island (Mauritius), where it is critically endangered in the wild with fewer than a few dozen specimens remaining.

directions: Plant in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, sandy or loamy soil in full sun in frost-free zone 10–11; spindle palm is closely related to bottle palm (both in the genus Hyophorbe), and while both have distinctively shaped trunks, spindle palm's trunk swells in the MIDDLE rather than at the base, creating a spindle or fusiform shape — widest in the center of the trunk and tapering toward both the base and the crownshaft. The taller, more slender crown of arching pinnate fronds and the graceful spindle-shaped trunk distinguish it clearly from the squat bottle palm. Like bottle palm, it is critically endangered in the wild (native to Rodrigues Island, Mauritius), but very widely cultivated as an ornamental throughout the tropical world. Slightly taller and more slender than bottle palm, with a more graceful overall form. Both Hyophorbe species are outstanding ornamentals for small garden spaces in frost-free climates where their compact size and architectural trunks are advantages.

planting.note: Plant seeds or container-grown palms in spring in well-drained to moist, slightly acidic to neutral, sandy or loamy soil in full sun; hurricane palm is extremely wind-resistant (hence the name), salt-tolerant, and fast-growing — ideal for exposed coastal sites in frost-free zones. Frost-sensitive — damaged below 30°F.

directions: Plant in well-drained to moist, slightly acidic to neutral, sandy or loamy soil in full sun in frost-free zone 10–11; hurricane palm is one of the most graceful and wind-tolerant single-trunk palms available for tropical and subtropical landscapes — a solitary-trunked palm from the Mascarene Islands (Mauritius, Réunion, Rodrigues) with a slender, elegant gray trunk and a relatively small crown of arching, pinnate fronds with distinctive reddish or purplish tinges on new growth, especially the new frond midribs and the underside of pinnae. The name 'hurricane palm' refers to its exceptional resistance to strong winds — the fronds survive wind damage that would destroy less flexible palms. Widely planted in South Florida, the Caribbean, Hawaii, and throughout tropical gardens worldwide as a graceful, medium-sized feature palm. Critically endangered in the wild (it is one of the 'palms of Mascarene' threatened by habitat loss and invasive species on its native islands), but commonly cultivated. Grows at a moderate to fast rate in warm, humid tropical conditions.
